coredata.dc:title: Sonochemical synthesis of zeolite NaP from clinoptilolite
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Behin, Jamshid
Kazemian, Hossein
Rohani, Sohrab
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, natural clinoptilolite was converted to zeolite NaP using ultrasonic energy, in which the transformation time shortened remarkably. The effect of post-synthesis treatment using conventional hydrothermal was also investigated. The synthesized powders were characterized by XRD, TGA/DTA, SEM, and PSD analysis. The results showed that, increasing the sonication time (energy) has no significant effect on the product’s morphology. The crystallinity of the synthesized samples increased slightly with increasing sonication time, but their yield remained relatively unchanged. Furthermore, post-synthesis hydrothermal treatment showed very little influence on properties of the final product. Because the ultrasonic irradiation creates acoustic cavitation cracks on the surface structure of clinoptilolite particulates and increases the concentration of soluble alumino-silicate species, which favors the prevailing super-saturation, crystallization and crystal growth of zeolite NaP happen faster. The particles of zeolite NaP synthesized by ultrasonic irradiation consist of small crystallites of uniform size.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Clinoptilolite
Crystallization
Rapid synthesis
Ultrasound
Zeolite NaP

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref
xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of different steps involved in US-assisted hydrothermal synthesize of zeolite NaP.
XRD patterns of untreated and hydrothermally treated clinoptilolite at 90°C after 5h and 72h (○: clinoptilolite characteristic peaks, ●: Zeolite NaP peaks).
SEM micrographs of (a) untreated and (b) hydrothermally treated clinoptilolite (at 90°C for 5h).

Comparison of XRD patterns of the natural and treated clinoptilolite by means of (a) US-assisted hydrothermal treatment (b) US-treatment (t son: sonication time, t hyd: hydrothermal treatment time, ○: clinoptilolite characteristic peaks, ●: NaP standard peaks).
Crystallization curve of conversion of natural clinoptilolite to zeolite NaP using US-assisted hydrothermal treatment versus sonication time.
Crystallization curve of conversion of natural clinoptilolite to zeolite NaP using US treatment versus sonication time (schematic presentation of zeolite re-crystallization).
Typical SEM photograph of crystal obtained by sonication alone.

XRD patterns of untreated and hydrothermally treated kaolin under 3h of sonication with NaF according to the reference [46] and with NaOH (i.e. similar to the method used in this article to convert clinoptilolite), (●: Kaloin standard peaks).
TGA/SDTA curves of untreated and sonochemically treated clinoptilolite.
Particle size distribution of (a) untreated and hydrothermally treated clinoptilolite at 90°C for 5h, (b) sonochemically treated clinoptilolite for 2, 3 and 4h.
Correlation of delivered energy versus sonication time.
Experimental runs at constant sonication power of 15W and temperature 90°C.
Chemical composition (XRF analysis) of the clinoptilolite and synthesis zeolite NaP.
Required energy calculations for US and US-assisted hydrothermal treatment.

head.ce:title: Sonochemical synthesis of zeolite NaP from clinoptilolite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biochemical Engineering, University of Western Ontario (UWO), London, Ontario, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biochemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, natural clinoptilolite was converted to zeolite NaP using ultrasonic energy, in which the transformation time shortened remarkably. The effect of post-synthesis treatment using conventional hydrothermal was also investigated. The synthesized powders were characterized by XRD, TGA/DTA, SEM, and PSD analysis. The results showed that, increasing the sonication time (energy) has no significant effect on the product’s morphology. The crystallinity of the synthesized samples increased slightly with increasing sonication time, but their yield remained relatively unchanged. Furthermore, post-synthesis hydrothermal treatment showed very little influence on properties of the final product. Because the ultrasonic irradiation creates acoustic cavitation cracks on the surface structure of clinoptilolite particulates and increases the concentration of soluble alumino-silicate species, which favors the prevailing super-saturation, crystallization and crystal growth of zeolite NaP happen faster. The particles of zeolite NaP synthesized by ultrasonic irradiation consist of small crystallites of uniform size.

ce:section.ce:para: Natural Clinoptilolite with an approximate chemical composition of (Ca, Fe, K, Mg, Na)4 Al6 Si30 O72 .24H2O, which is considered as one of the most abundant natural zeolite on the earth has a Heulandite (HEU) type framework [1–4]. The commercial application of this natural micro-porous mineral is mainly limited to animal husbandry, agricultural and water purification processes. Relatively low cation exchange capacity and low zeolitic phase purity could be considered as the main drawbacks associated with zeolitic minerals. These drawbacks limit the large scale commercial applications of natural zeolites, particularly, in water treatment and gas purifications applications. High purity synthetic zeolites with higher adsorption capacity and well-defined crystalline structure are used in industrial processes rather than their natural zeolite counterparts. High quality synthetic zeolites are synthesized using a wide range of silicon and aluminum sources including natural aluminosilicates such as clay minerals and natural zeolites as well as industrial grade chemicals [5,6]. Clinoptilolite can be considered as a good candidate as a starting material for producing synthetic zeolite such as NaP with much higher cation exchange capacity.

Zeolite NaP with Gismondine (GIS) framework type is one of the most interesting synthesized zeolites with high cation exchange capacity. Due to small pore size of this micro-porous material, it is useful for gas separation application, removal of heavy metals, ammonium, toxic and radioactive waste species, sea water potassium extraction, water-softening and as a builder and water-softening agent for eco-friendly detergent [7,8]. Conventional, microwave-assisted, and ultrasound-assisted hydrothermal methods are used to synthesis zeolite NaP from various alumina and silica precursors [9,10].

There are plenty of reports on conventional hydrothermal synthesis of zeolite NaP from different sources such as natural clinoptilolite [11–14], inorganic precursor solution [7,8,15], zeolite A [16], zeolite Y [17,18], coal fly ash [19–30], and agricultural waste materials [31]. The conventional hydrothermal method is an energy- and time-consuming process, in which the crystallization time might be as long as 8–100h. Furthermore, successful crystallization happens at relatively high temperature up to 200°C.

Microwave radiation (MW) and ultrasound (US) energy could be considered as alternative promising energy sources to shorten zeolite crystallization time substantially. MW and US assisted synthesis of zeolitic porous materials offer a simple and fast method in comparison to the conventional hydrothermal techniques [32–38]. It has been suggested that using microwave and ultrasound as energy sources could lead to homogenous nucleation and uniform distribution of thermal energy and decrease the reaction time remarkably, which make zeolitization an economically viable process.

There are also several reports on microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesize of zeolite NaP from different sources such as pure precursor [39,40], and coal fly ash [41]. Microwave energy can reduce the crystallization time from days to hours and even minutes depending on the batch composition and reaction condition including temperature and pressure.

The ultrasound is known as an effective source of energy for changing the chemical properties of substances and accelerating chemical processes. Ultrasound is safer compared to microwave because its wave frequencies are lower than the microwave [42]. The chemical effects of ultrasound originate from acoustic cavitation and collapsing of generated micro-bubbles in liquid medium, which leads to a micro-mixing effect. This phenomenon increases the secondary nucleation rates and enhances mass transfer that eventually increases crystal growth rates [42]. Askari et al. [43] reviewed the effect of ultrasound on the synthesis of zeolites A, ZSM-5, SAPO-34, MCM-22 and MCM-49. Recently, Pal et al. [9] synthesized zeolite NaP from pure chemicals by sonochemical method and compared the results with conventional hydrothermal technique. The initial mixture of precursors was stirred at room temperature for 1h and then sonicated for 3h. During the sonication, the temperature of bulk reaction mixture raised up to 80°C. According to their study, sonochemical treatment followed by hydrothermal treatment does not show any significant effect on the crystallization and phase formation of zeolite under the studied condition. In another work, Azizi et al. [44] synthesized zeolite NaP under ultrasonic and microwave ageing process prior to the hydrothermal crystallization using perlite as the source of silica and alumina. The optimum conditions for maximum zeolite crystallinity (97%) were obtained at 55min microwave-assisted ageing and 4h of hydrothermal crystallization time.

To the best of the authors’ knowledge, the sonochemical conversion of clinoptilolite into zeolite NaP is not reported. In this study, natural clinoptilolite was converted into zeolite NaP by means of ultrasound irradiation in an alkali medium as a facile method of synthesis. Furthermore, the effectiveness of ultrasound (US) and US-assisted hydrothermal method are compared.

The starting material used in this research was a natural clinoptilolite with a particle size of less than 250μm without any chemical pre-treatment (ZeoInc Company, USA). Analytical grade sodium hydroxide (Alphachem, Canada) was used as received without further purification. Kaolin light (BDH Merck, UK) and sodium fluoride (Sigma-Aldrich, USA) were used for supplementary experiments. De-ionized (DI) water was used for making solutions.
The procedure of the ultrasound-assisted hydrothermal synthesize of zeolite NaP from natural clinoptilolite involves three main steps as is shown in Fig. 1 .

In first step, alkali solution was prepared by dissolving 1.2g of NaOH into 30mL of deionized water to form 1N solution. For each experiment, 2g of clinoptilolite were added to 30mL of the alkali solution. Then, the mixture was subjected to ultrasound radiation at different durations to study the effect of irradiation time on crystallization of zeolite NaP. A jacketed cylindrical glass reactor (i.e. ID of 4cm and length of 10cm) was used to conduct the reaction inside the ultrasound chamber under controlled temperature condition using a circulating water bath. The crystallization experiments were carried out using an ultrasonic processor (Sonics, Vibra-Cell, USA) with a standard probe (i.e. Tip diameter of 13mm) for sonicating under atmospheric pressure. The ultrasound processor produces acoustic waves at the frequency of 20kHz. The delivered power of ultrasound wave (35% max) was 15W. During sonication, the temperature of mixture raised from ambient temperature to a steady state of 90°C within 5min. Using a circulating water bath, the reactor temperature was kept constant at 90°C (±1°C). The photographs of the synthesis setup and the jacketed glass reactor are illustrated in Fig. 2 .

In the second step, the mixture of the previous step was subjected to the hydrothermal synthesis in a US-assisted hydrothermal treatment. In the US treatment alone, however, the hydrothermal treatment step was bypassed and mixture subjected to filtration, washing and drying. To compare the effects of US-assisted hydrothermal and sonication synthesis techniques, two sets of experiments were designed and conducted. In the first set of experiments for US-assisted hydrothermal treatment, after a given period of US irradiation, the sonicated products were subjected to hydrothermal treatment for various times depending on the sonication time, in which the total synthesis time was 5h. The sonication time varied between zero (no sonication) to 5h and hydrothermal post-treatment was performed in the way that total treatment time was constant for all samples. In other word, for sonication time of 5h no hydrothermal post-treatment was applied. Hydrothermal treatment was carried out in an end-over-end autoclave oven under controlled temperature. The temperature in sonication and hydrothermal steps was constant at a value of 90°C (±1°C). In the second set of experiments, the initial mixture was subjected to US irradiation alone without hydrothermal post-treatment. After sonication step, the solid product was directly subjected to the third step. The list of experimental runs including sonochemical and hydrothermal treatments is presented in Table 1 . The reaction time of 5h was chosen to ensure the crystallization is complete and no further evolution observed for yield of produced zeolite NaP. At the end of thermo-sonically activated reaction, the reactor was cooled down to room temperature.

In the third step, the synthesized zeolite was filtered, washed thoroughly with deionized water to remove un-reacted and water soluble components, dried overnight at 100°C (±1°C) using an electric oven, and then characterized by means of different instrumental techniques.

A Rigaku-MiniFlex powder diffractometer (Japan) was used to collect XRD data of the synthesized samples using CuKα (λ =1.54059Å) over the range of 5°<2θ <40° with step width of 0.02°. The XRD patterns of the synthesized zeolite samples compared with the characteristic peaks of NaP from references [45]. The crystallinity of the products was determined by “peak fitting” algorithm in the MDI-Jade v 7.5 software. Crystal size distribution and morphology of zeolite were studied by scanning electron microscope (SEM) using a JSM 600F (Joel, Japan) operating at 10keV of acceleration voltage. The thermal analyses were performed using a Mettler Toledo TGA/DTA 851e model (Switzerland) with version 6.1 Stare software where the samples were heated from 30°C to 600°C at heating rate of 10°C/min under nitrogen purge of 40ml/min. Mastersizer 2000 (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK) was used for particle size distribution (PSD) analysis.

In order to find a proper batch composition, preliminary experiments conducted by converting the natural clinoptilolite to zeolite NaP using conventional hydrothermal treatment at 90°C for 5h. Raw natural clinoptilolite was first characterized. The XRD pattern of untreated clinoptilolite (Fig. 3 ) showed at least three strong main peaks at 2θ: 9.9, 22.4 and 30.1° [45,46]. The XRD pattern of zeolite NaP obtained by hydrothermal treatment of clinoptilolite at 90°C for 72h is also shown as a reference. Its main characteristic peaks were at 2θ: 12.5, 21.7, 28.1 and 33.4° [45,46]. The XRD pattern obtained from hydrothermally treated clinoptilolite after 5h at 90°C did not show any characteristic peaks related to the formation of zeolite NaP. As it can be seen, there is no significant profile change for treated sample, suggesting that hydrothermal treatment for 5h at 90°C could not induce the conversion of natural clinoptilolite into zeolite NaP. Under the same reactions condition, further prolonging the reaction time to 7h did not show any sign of formation of zeolite NaP.

Fig. 4 illustrates the SEM images of untreated and hydrothermally treated clinoptilolite at 90°C for 5h. Natural clinoptilolite shows existence of a large amount of amorphous phase of undefined morphology with rough surface. Particles are homogeneous in micro-scale and possess rough external surfaces with a shape of irregular flakes (lamellar texture with separate tin plate or tin bar crystalline form on the surface). These photographs confirm no significant change after the hydrothermal treatment.

The experiments were carried out in two steps, in which ultrasound irradiation under atmospheric pressure was followed by a conventional hydrothermal treatment under autogenesis pressure using an autoclave reactor in an end-over-end oven. The clinoptilolite was subjected to US-irradiation and then conventional hydrothermal treatment in such a way that total treatment time was kept constant for 5h at 90°C. The sonication time, however, was varied between 0.5 and 5h. In Fig. 5 a, XRD patterns of the US-assisted synthesized NaP zeolites are compared with the parent natural clinoptilolite. It is clear that after 1h of sonication and 4h of hydrothermal crystallization, zeolite NaP was formed, in which characteristic peaks at 2θ: 12.5, 21.7, 28.1 and 33.4°, ascertain the formation of a new zeolitic phase. It can be concluded that under the studied condition, the conversion of clinoptilolite to zeolite NaP took place without having any intermediate meta-stable phase. It has been reported that ultrasonic energy helps to improve yields, rates and properties of products of a chemical reaction. In this reaction, clinoptilolite partially dissolved under ultrasonic irradiation at elevated temperature to form an amorphous gel. Then nucleation of NaP from this aluminosilicates gel begins on the surface of un-dissolved zeolite particulates and the rate of crystal growth increases under hydrothermal treatment at controlled temperature [43,9]. The crystallization of zeolite started earlier by using ultrasound and also a significant increase in nucleation rate and a decrease in indication time were observed. By using US-assisted hydrothermal treatment, the zeolitic phase transformation occurred faster and re-crystallization was completed in 5h. These results show a significant reduction in crystallization time and induction period by application of the ultrasonic irradiation. US-assisted hydrothermal treatment increases the zeolite NaP crystal growth rate by increasing the concentration of soluble silicate and aluminate precursors needed for formation of NaP. The acoustic cavitation provides enough activation energy to collapse clinoptilolite structure and increases the concentration of soluble Si and Al species that are needed for re-crystallization and growth of NaP zeolitic phase [43]. By increasing the sonication time from 1 to 5h and decreasing the hydrothermal treatment time from 4 to 0h, a relatively constant intensity of NaP peaks is emerged in the product. It can be concluded that sonochemical energy is the dominant factor that controls zeolite transformation under the studied condition.

Fig. 6 illustrates the yield of the synthesized zeolite NaP (i.e. NaP/clinoptilolite ratio) and the crystallinity of the product versus sonication time. The NaP/clinoptilolite ratio was calculated based on the area under the main characteristic peaks. Three main peaks of zeolite NaP at the 2θ of 12.5, 28.1, 33.4°, and three main peaks of clinoptilolite at the 2θ of 9.9, 22.4, 30.1° were used for this calculation. The areas corresponding to each peak was determined by Jade v 7.5 software, then the sum of areas corresponding to each phase (i.e. NaP and clinoptilolite) was calculated. NaP/clinoptilolite ratio is defined by the sum of NaP peaks areas to the sum of areas corresponding to NaP and clinoptilolite phases together. These experimental data show that the kinetics of crystallization (re-crystallization) of zeolite NaP is enhanced under ultrasonic condition compared to the conventional conditions. This means NaP zeolite product with higher yield and higher crystallinity is obtained with ultrasonically treated sample. It is noteworthy that under the US-assisted treatment conditions tested in this research, the product consisted of a high purity NaP zeolitic phase. Although the exact mechanism of this enhanced kinetics is not known in detail, it is generally accepted that chemical activation and process intensification are provided through ultrasonically induced cavitation developing local high temperature and pressure in a liquid medium [47]. Synthesis of zeolite NaP product with about 70% crystallinity is achieved in at least 1h ultrasonic process followed by 4h conventional hydrothermal treatment at 90°C and overall reaction time of 5h. This is noteworthy that our preliminary test revealed that under the same reaction condition (i.e. same temperature and batch composition), no conversion was observed within 7h by means of conventional hydrothermal treatment without ultrasonic irradiation.

In this set of experiments, ultrasonic energy was used as the only source of energy to convert natural clinoptilolite to NaP zeolite. The XRD patterns of samples after sonochemical treatment are presented in Fig. 5b. The characteristic peaks of zeolite NaP phase were first detected after 3h of sonication. The XRD patterns of zeolite NaP after 4 and 5h of sonication indicate that the peaks intensities of samples were the same as those sonicated for 3h, which means the ultrasound irradiation has a little influence on the structure of synthesized zeolite. Further treatment time did not influence the peaks intensities.

As it is shown in Fig. 7 , the amount of zeolite NaP in the product increased from zero percent to more than 80% after only 3h and reached to a maximum after 5h of sonochemical treatment. Prolonging of sonication time has a little influence on the yield (from 90% to 100%). Reducing the synthesis time to 3h was sufficient to get relatively high yield NaP zeolite. NaP phase starts to appear after 3h with 58% sample crystallinity. After 5h of sonication, more than 70% crystallinity of zeolite NaP was achieved. Further prolongation of crystallization time did not result remarkable improvement of the XRD peaks.

The SEM images of US-treated clinoptilolite, just before (i.e. after 2.5h of sonication) and after transformation (3h and 5h of sonication), are displayed in Fig. 8 . These micrographs demonstrate that because of sonication, the morphology of sample is changed compared to the untreated parent clinoptilolite (see Fig. 4). Given the SEM image of the untreated clinoptilolite, the destruction of clinoptilolite structure under sonication could be considered as a factor that facilitates partial dissolution of sample. Indeed, a macro-porous texture is observed on the surface of particle after 2.5h of sonication (Fig. 8a). Collapsing of bubbles during cavitation produces hot points with very high temperature and pressure that will promote the dissolution of silicate in a very short time. The crystallization (re-crystallization) happened in 0.5h immediately after super-saturation of the synthesis mixture and gel formation (Fig. 8b). The spherical shape crystals are identified as zeolite NaP, which are similar to those reported in the literature [17,39,40]. The pure NaP phase exhibits a pseudo-spherical morphology (spherulitic crystal aggregate and ball-shape cluster of zeolite NaP), because the spheres are actually formed by small plates (rounded octahedron with concave faces). These micrographs clearly show that both NaP samples (sonicated for 3 and 5h) are well crystallized, although the degree of crystallinity and the size of crystals show no significant difference, under ultrasonic conditions. No significant difference was observed using US-irradiation as it was already confirmed by XRD data. Sonication time longer than 3h causes very small increase in the yield of zeolite NaP, which suggests that longer sonication time may not have significant effect on the quality of the final product. It is noteworthy that the size distribution becomes narrower and the shape of crystals become more homogenous with US-treatment because of a high nucleation rate, which is faster than the crystal growth rate [48].

Based on the SEM micrographs of the zeolitic products at various synthesis times (i.e. 3 and 5h), a slight increase of crystal size by sonication time can be concluded. Ultrasound would increase the rate of crystal growth because of an enhanced mass transfer resulting from micro-mixing phenomenon. It was found that the synthesized crystals by ultrasound are smaller compared to those by hydrothermal synthesis. Crystallization with ultrasound results in a crystallinity between 58% and 75% for a low given treatment time (from 3 to 5h). From XRD and SEM images it was found that the optimum sonication time is 3h at 90°C.

The exact mechanism of crystallization under ultrasonic energy is yet unknown. Nevertheless, ultrasound generates high-energy shock waves that can significantly change morphology and size distribution. The observed effects of sonication on the crystal size and morphology are attributed to the increase in the nucleation rate. Cavitation bubbles, act as nuclei and collapsing of these bubbles occurs in less than a nanosecond, which causes super-saturation. Ultrasound also provides an acoustic stream that passes through the liquid and solid media and its stirring effect decreases the diffusion layer thickness thereby improving the mass transfer dramatically. Ultrasound also affects depolymerization–polymerization equilibrium through increasing the concentration of soluble species, which is necessary for the formation of zeolite.

Chemical composition of the natural clinoptilolite used as the starting material and synthesized zeolite NaP by US treatment (3h) are summarized in Table 2 . According to the elemental analysis data, the SiO2/Al2O3 ratio of the natural clinoptilolite and the corresponding synthesized zeolite NaP was found to be 5.44 and 2.82, respectively. Considering that the original material has a Si/Al ratio of 5.4 of the starting natural zeolite, the destruction process of the clinoptilolite involves a partial dissolution of the sample, with preferential incorporation of silicon into the liquid phase. At a large quantity of silicate ion in the liquid phase, aluminate ions and silicate ions are condensed to form an aluminosilicate gel. At a moderate concentration of sodium hydroxide (low Na2O/SiO2 ratio), and depending on temperature, the amorphous gel of lower Si/A1 ratio (equivalent to 3) creates immediately zeolite NaP phase until the aluminates ions (as limiting reactant) remaining in alkali solution are consumed [11–13,26,9]. Therefore, it can be concluded that increasing the dissolution rate of constituents leads to the rapid nucleation that might be the main effect of ultrasound irradiation on the re-crystallization process.

Supplementary experiments were performed based on the verified methods of analysis published by the International Zeolite Association (IZA) on the synthesis of NaP zeolite from kaolin as a source of Al and Si [46]. The standard solution with batch composition of Al2O3: 2.2 SiO2: 5.28 NaF: 105.6 H2O was prepared. Conventional hydrothermal method takes 60days to crystallize to zeolite NaP. Furthermore, another batch of kaolin was treated with NaOH similar to that of clinoptilolite. Both batches were subjected to US irradiation at 90°C at different reaction time, then filtered, washed and dried. For both samples, the XRD patterns after 3h sonication are shown in Fig. 9 . It is clear that no significant change was observed after sonochemical treatment and no zeolite NaP was formed. In the synthesis process with standard solution, the peak intensities corresponding to kaolinite at 2θ of 12.6 and 25.1° were decreased, whereas an unknown peak started to appear at 2θ of 39°.

The sonication time and operating temperature in this study are similar to a work published by Pal et al. [9] except that they synthesized zeolite NaP from pure chemical sources. They synthesized zeolite NaP after 3h of sonication time from silica and aluminum-tri-sec-butylate with molar composition 4Na2O: 0.2 Al2O3: 1SiO2: 200H2O. They found that the crystallinity of the powder reduced gradually with increasing irradiation time and rapid heating/cooling rates during bubble evolution leading to a reduction of the nano particle crystallinity up to the formation of an amorphous structure. It should be noted that the synthesized product in this study was not pure zeolite NaP, but a mixture of zeolite NaP and unconverted clinoptilolite. Therefore, the product crystallinity comprises the crystallinity of the binary phases which was determined by Jade software. The crystallinity of clinoptilolite phase decreased and silicon and aluminum dissolved from clinoptilolite up to 3h of sonication, where the first peaks of zeolite NaP were observed. Clinoptilolite continued to provide the Si and Al ions into the liquid as a source of aluminate and silicate and increased crystal growth of zeolite NaP slightly, which is different from the observation during the synthesis of zeolite NaP phase from pure chemical. The irradiation was stopped when the peaks indicating clinoptilolite phase totally disappeared and the peaks corresponding to zeolite NaP reached to the highest intensity, (i.e. after 5h of sonication). The effect of US irradiation time on crystallinity and crystal growth of zeolite NaP after reaching to a maximum yield (i.e. NaP/clinoptilolite ratio) was not studied in this work; however, similar studies are reported by Pal et al. [9].

The water content of zeolites is an important factor for most of their applications and thermal profile of each zeolite is a characteristic parameter. The TGA analysis was done as a supplementary test for further verification of the conversion process. Using this TGA curve, one can distinguish the difference between the maximum water release temperature of natural clinoptilolite (around 100°C) and zeolite Na-P (around 150°C). The TGA/DTA curves of natural clinoptilolite and synthesized zeolite NaP are shown in Fig. 10 . The TGA curve of the NaP zeolite synthesized via conventional hydrothermal technique (as Reference) for 72h from clinoptilolite is shown in Fig. 10. This TGA curve is similar to the curve of zeolite synthesized by sonication, which means thermal stability and water content of the two synthesized zeolites using different sources of energies are the same. The thermal analysis results revealed that the overall thermal behavior of the samples is similar to those reported in the literature [40,49]. The weight loss as a result of heating from room temperature up to 600°C was 10% and 15% for natural clinoptilolite and NaP products, respectively. The DTA shows peaks at 100 and 150°C, which means the maximum weight loss for natural and synthetic zeolite happens at these temperature, respectively. In the case of NaP sample, however, these is a wide peak at 100°C as well, that can be attributed to the loss of the adsorbed moisture and the second peak at (150°C) may be attributed to the evaporation of water trapped in the zeolite cage due to fast polymerization [17,9]. On the other hand, untreated clinoptilolite and sonicated clinoptilolite for 2.5h show continuous dehydration in TGA/SDTA curves up to 600°C [49]. Zeolite NaP synthesized hydrothermally for 72h has only 2% higher capacity to adsorb moisture.

The particle size distribution (PSD) analysis of the untreated and treated clinoptilolite is shown in Fig. 11 . It confirms that hydrothermal treatment for 5h has no significant influence on the particle size distribution (Fig. 11a). The un-treated sample has a particle size ranging from 5 to 50μm with an average size of 14.07μm. An approximate shift of 7.8% toward larger average size (i.e. 15.17μm) is observed in the PSD curve of the hydrothermally treated sample. Data presented in Fig. 11b shows the effect of sonication on the particle size distribution of three samples. The particle size distribution became wider with prolonging the sonication time. After sonication, the particle size distribution curve moved towards larger particle sizes. A mean particle sizes of 49.05 and 60.4μm were obtained over a period of 2 and 4h sonication, respectively. This trend is in agreement with literature [50]. The US-treatment results in the formation of zeolite NaP with a non-Gaussian distribution curve. Although using ultrasonication for decreasing the particle size of a solid by collapsing the structure or de-agglomeration phenomenon is well-known, however, in these experimental data an increase in the particle size was observed that can be attributed to the increased growth rate.

Although a shorter synthesis time is desirable, the amount of energy that is consumed with different techniques, is also crucial. The energy consumption in the sonication stage versus sonication time is plotted in Fig. 12 . The energy consumption increases during sonication period and has a linear relation with time (R 2 =0.996). From the energy versus sonication time plot, it can be concluded that at 90°C, an activation energy of at least 75kJ/g of clinoptilolite is required to re-crystallize it to NaP zeolite. This low energy requirement is one of the advantages of ultrasound compared to other methods, particularly conventional hydrothermal technique.

Table 3 shows a short investigation on the energy consumption for both US and US-assisted hydrothermal treatments to better understand the economically convenient case of the zeolite NaP synthesis. Energy consumption for hydrothermal treatment was calculated based on convective heat transfer in reactor jacket. The heat performance of water bath was assumed to be 100% (no heat loss in water bath). Comparison of the total energy consumption (E total) per unit mass of synthesized zeolite NaP for both US and US-assisted hydrothermal treatments suggested that 3h sonication would be the optimum economical time for synthesis procedure. Therefore, the sonication technique is extremely promising in the crystallization processes since it can initiate nuclei and control subsequent crystal growth in a saturated medium compared to the hydrothermal technique. It can be concluded that sonochemistry is a facile, fast and energy saving crystallization process that can be used for process intensification toward converting clinoptilolite into zeolite NaP.

Zeolite NaP, with much higher cation exchange capacity compared to the natural clinoptilolite, is successfully synthesized in 3h under ultrasonic radiation using natural clinoptilolite as the sources of Si and Al. Much faster transformation of zeolite framework from HEU (i.e. clinoptilolite) to GIS (i.e. NaP) can be due to the shock waves induced by ultrasound radiation. The collapsing of micro-bubbles that are produced by ultrasound waves provides enough activation energy to transform clinoptilolite into zeolite NaP in an alkali medium. Further hydrothermal treatments had no significant effects on the properties of formed zeolite but slightly increased the crystallinity. Because of low energy consumption and very short synthesis time of sonochemical method compared to the hydrothermal method, it should be considered as an economic viable approach to transform zeolitic frameworks. Concluding remarks of this research can be summarized as following:
–
Ultrasonic irradiation accelerated the re-crystallization process of conversion natural clinoptilolite to synthetic NaP zeolite. Given the short reaction time and low energy consumption, ultrasound-based zeolitization process has a potential to be used for converting clinoptilolite to zeolite NaP at larger scales.
–
Application of ultrasound can influence the size and morphology of the crystallites, crystallization time and the crystallinity of the final zeolitic product. Crystallites of the zeolite NaP synthesized under ultrasonic treatment at lower temperature are homogeneously distributed. The SEM analysis did not show any significant variation in crystallite size for conversion of clinoptilolite with different sonication time. The effect of ultrasonic energy on nucleation rate is related to surface imperfections. Moreover, the use of ultrasound changes the particle size distribution of the final product.
